questions.prompt: What's the fundamental difference between gas generator and full-flow staged combustion cycles?

choices.text: Gas generator dumps turbopump exhaust overboard after using it to spin the turbines, losing 1-5% of propellant to no thrust contribution (~5% Isp penalty). Full-flow staged combustion routes ALL propellant — both fuel-rich and oxidizer-rich pre-burner exhausts — back into the main combustion chamber, so everything contributes to thrust. FFSC gets higher Isp (typically 30-50s improvement), enables higher chamber pressure, and runs turbopumps at lower temperatures. The cost is dramatic complexity — two pre-burners, intricate plumbing, decades of engineering development, and serious ox-rich combustion risks. Raptor is the first FFSC engine to fly to space.

questions.explanation: The cycle determines whether turbopump-driving propellant gets used twice (for pumps and for thrust) or just once. FFSC's 'use everything' approach is more efficient but vastly more complex.

questions.prompt: Why did SpaceX use gas generator for Merlin and full-flow staged combustion for Raptor — different cycles for two SpaceX engines?

choices.text: Different missions, different optimal cycles. Merlin was developed in the mid-2000s when SpaceX needed working engines fast and Falcon 9 needed competitive (not extraordinary) Isp for LEO and GTO work — gas generator was well-understood and quick to develop. Raptor was developed for Starship's full reusability, methane fuel for Mars-relevance, and high-energy missions where every second of Isp matters across the full-stack reuse cycle — FFSC's higher Isp, better restart, and higher chamber pressure all directly help Starship's economics. The 8+ years of Raptor development was an explicit bet that maximum performance was worth the complexity for Starship's design space.

questions.explanation: Cycle choice tracks vehicle requirements. Workhorse 2-stage rocket with reasonable Isp targets: gas generator. Maximum-performance, full-reusable, Mars-class vehicle: FFSC. The same company can make both choices coherently because the constraints differ.
